title: Chapter 4: Equations, Inequalities and Graphs — Medium

questions.question: Solve the equation $|3x - 1| = 2x + 6$.
questions.explanation:
Case 1: $3x - 1 = 2x + 6 \implies x = 7$. Checking $x=7$ gives $2(7)+6 = 20 \ge 0$ (Valid). Case 2: $3x - 1 = -(2x + 6) \implies 5x = -5 \implies x = -1$. Checking $x=-1$ gives $2(-1)+6 = 4 \ge 0$ (Valid). Thus, both solutions are valid.

**Common mistake:** Students may fail to check for extraneous solutions but also occasionally reject perfectly valid negative $x$ values, misremembering that it is the right-hand side expression $g(x)$ that cannot be negative, not $x$ itself.

questions.question: Find the complete set of values of $x$ for which $|2x - 3| < 9$.
questions.explanation:
Rewrite the inequality as $-9 < 2x - 3 < 9$. Adding^3 to all parts yields $-6 < 2x < 12$. Dividing by^2 gives the solution interval $-3 < x < 6$.

**Common mistake:** Students often treat the problem as a standard equation boundary match, but mess up the direction of the inequality flip for the negative boundary, ending up with outer intervals.

questions.question: Solve the inequality $|3 - 2x| \ge 7$.
questions.explanation:
This splits into two separate linear inequalities: $3 - 2x \ge 7 \implies -2x \ge 4 \implies x \le -2$; and $3 - 2x \le -7 \implies -2x \le -10 \implies x \ge 5$. Combining these yields $x \le -2$ or $x \ge 5$.

**Common mistake:** Students frequently forget to flip the inequality sign when dividing by the negative coefficient $-2$, resulting in a completely inverted or bounded region like $-2 \le x \le 5$.
